speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back to Git Going with GitHub. This is episode 29: GitHub Security Features. I am Alex, and today we are turning GitHub Security Features from a list of instructions into a working mental model.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And I am Jamie. I will stop us whenever the instructions sound simple on paper but might feel different with a keyboard and screen reader.

speaker: ALEX
text: Dependabot, secret scanning, code scanning, and private security advisories. That is the surface description. Underneath it, we are building judgment: where to focus, what to ignore, and how to verify the result.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So we are not using the audio as a shortcut around learning. We are using it to make the learning easier to enter.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. A good audio lesson gives someone enough context to try the work with confidence, even before they open the written material.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay, set the room for us. What are we walking into?

speaker: ALEX
text: Dependabot, Secret Scanning, Code Scanning, and Private Advisories: Who this is for: Anyone contributing to open source repositories needs to understand how GitHub protects code and what security alerts mean. The next useful detail is concrete: This appendix explains the GitHub Security and quality tab, how to interpret and respond to alerts, and how to responsibly report vulnerabilities - including in community-access/accessibility-agents.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid pull request habit is to separate three questions: what changed, why it changed, and what still needs review. That keeps the conversation useful instead of noisy.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How would you walk the room through that step by step?

speaker: ALEX
text: Quick Navigation has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: The Security and quality tab - What It Contains. Then: Dependabot - Automated Dependency Updates. Next: Secret Scanning - Preventing Credential Leaks. Last: Code Scanning and CodeQL. The point is not speed; the point is never losing your place.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does the learner do first, second, and then after that?

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Private Vulnerability Reporting; The SECURITY.md File; Software Bill of Materials (SBOM); and Screen Reader Navigation of the Security and quality tab. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: ALEX
text: A solid navigation habit is to prove where you are before activating controls. Headings, landmarks, and the address bar are not trivia; they are your map.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the one idea that makes the next few steps less mysterious?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside 1. The Security and quality tab - What It Contains: every GitHub repository has a Security and quality tab in its navigation bar. That matters in practice: What you see there depends on whether you are a contributor with elevated access or a public viewer.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That feels much more doable when you say it as one move.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The magic is not speed. The magic is knowing what changed and why it matters.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now bring the learner back to the room. Public viewers see has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: For a learner, the useful signals are concrete. Security policy (if the repo has a SECURITY.md). The private vulnerability reporting form (if enabled by the maintainer).

speaker: JAMIE
text: What would you say to someone who is already bracing for this to be too much?

speaker: ALEX
text: Contributors with write or admin access see has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The parts worth keeping in working memory are these. Dependabot alerts. Secret scanning alerts. Code scanning alerts. Security advisories. The ability to manage security settings.

speaker: ALEX
text: The useful habit is simple: orient, act, verify, then continue. That pause between action and trust is part of the work.

speaker: ALEX
text: That matters because of the next idea. Learning Cards: The Security and quality tab has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: On the ground, that means a few things. Use G then S (GitHub keyboard shortcut) to jump directly to the Security and quality tab from any repo page -- much faster than Tab-navigating the full nav bar. The Security and quality tab content varies by your access level -- public viewers see only the security policy and vulnerability reporting form; contributors with write access see alerts. Dependabot alerts, secret scanning alerts, and code scanning alerts are each a separate link inside the Security and quality tab -- use K to navigate between them. The Security and quality tab link sits in the repository's top navigation bar alongside Code, Issues, PRs, and Actions -- zoom in if the tab labels are small. Alert severity badges (Critical, High, Medium, Low) use colored labels -- each badge also includes the severity word, so color is not the only indicator. If the Security and quality tab shows "No alerts," that is good news -- look for the green shield icon next to the tab name as confirmation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If I am listening before the workshop starts, what should settle in my mind first?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, 2. Dependabot - Automated Dependency Updates is still useful because dependabot is GitHub's automated dependency monitoring and update system.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the talk moves from concept to action. Put Dependabot Alerts into plain language. When a known security vulnerability (CVE - Common Vulnerability and Exposure) is discovered in a package your project depends on, GitHub creates a Dependabot alert.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now it sounds like a workflow instead of a wall of instructions.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is where confidence comes from: not from never getting lost, but from knowing how to recover.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Turn that into a path someone can follow.

speaker: ALEX
text: What to do as a contributor when you see an alert has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 4 checks: Read the advisory to understand the vulnerability scope; Check whether the project actually uses the vulnerable code path; The fix is almost always: update the dependency to the fixed version; and Dependabot Security Updates may have already opened a PR - check the PRs tab. Pause after each step and listen for the confirmation before moving on.

speaker: ALEX
text: Before the learner moves on. This part earns its place because if enabled, Dependabot automatically opens a PR to update the vulnerable dependency. That gives the learner a foothold: as a contributor with access, reviewing and merging these Dependabot PRs is a high-value, low-risk way to contribute. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the version that sounds like an instructor, not a manual.

speaker: ALEX
text: Dependabot Version Updates: Beyond security fixes, Dependabot can be configured to open PRs keeping all dependencies at their latest versions (not just security fixes). The next useful detail is concrete: This is configured in.github/dependabot.yml.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now slow down for the part people usually miss. Here is the learner-facing version. Secret scanning detects if you accidentally commit tokens, API keys, passwords, or other credentials to a repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Where do you want a learner to place their attention here?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside Push Protection (Real-Time Prevention): push Protection intercepts a git push before it reaches GitHub and blocks it if it detects a known secret pattern.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is the kind of detail that keeps a screen reader user oriented.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The named thing - the heading, tab, field, branch, or button - is the handhold.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What is the ordered workflow?

speaker: ALEX
text: Anchor this part on What to do. Best practice: Use environment variables for secrets, never hardcode them. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of this as 3 checks: Remove the secret from the file immediately; Rotate the exposed credential (GitHub will automatically revoke detected GitHub tokens); and If it was a false positive, you can bypass with justification - but investigate first. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Secret Scanning Alerts. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: for public repositories, GitHub scans all existing commits and creates alerts for any detected secrets. The listener should be able to check this: These appear in Security → Secret scanning.

speaker: ALEX
text: The next point gives the learner a handle. Learning Cards: Secret Scanning has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is where the lesson becomes something you can check. If your git push is blocked with "GH013: Repository rule violations found," the terminal output lists the exact file, line number, and secret type -- read the error lines carefully. After removing the secret from your code, use environment variables instead (process.env.GITHUB TOKEN) -- your screen reader will confirm the variable name in the code. Secret scanning alerts appear under Security and quality tab, Secret scanning -- navigate with T to reach the alert table, then arrow keys to read each row. Push Protection error messages appear in your terminal with red text -- increase terminal font size to read the file path, line number, and secret type clearly. The secret scanning alert list in the Security and quality tab uses a table layout -- zoom in to read the Secret type and Location columns. Environment variable names in code (like process.env.GITHUB TOKEN) are easier to spot when syntax highlighting is enabled in your editor theme.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on 4. Code Scanning and CodeQL. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: If the interface shifts, 4. Code Scanning and CodeQL is still useful because code scanning uses static analysis to find security vulnerabilities in the code itself (not dependencies). For someone navigating by keyboard or screen reader, this detail matters: GitHub's built-in tool is CodeQL, which understands the code's logic and can detect.

speaker: ALEX
text: A few details make that real. SQL injection risks. Cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities. Path traversal issues. Insecure cryptography usage.

speaker: ALEX
text: Hold that next to this. Put Reading a Code Scanning Alert into plain language. As a contributor: Code scanning alerts are excellent, well-scoped contribution opportunities. The useful version is: Each alert shows you the exact file, line, and the data flow causing the issue. The interface gets easier when it becomes a set of named places instead of a wall of controls.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the learner is not behind if they stop there and check the page.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Pausing to verify is not a detour; it is how you keep control of the workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Where to Find Code Scanning Alerts. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Security and quality tab → Code scanning → filter by severity, rule, or file.

speaker: ALEX
text: That connects to another useful point. This part earns its place because when you discover a security vulnerability in a project, never report it as a public issue. That gives the learner a foothold: a public issue immediately broadcasts the vulnerability to anyone who could exploit it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Give me the sequence, because order matters here.

speaker: ALEX
text: How to Submit a Private Report has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is what that changes in practice. Severity: Your assessment. Affected versions: Which versions are vulnerable. CVSS score: Optional - the Common Vulnerability Scoring System rating.

speaker: ALEX
text: First, navigate to the repository's Security and quality tab. Then, select "Report a vulnerability" (this button only appears if the maintainer has enabled private reporting). After that, fill in the form. Finally, submit - only the maintainers see your report. That is the rhythm: orient, act, verify, continue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on The Responsible Disclosure Process. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The Responsible Disclosure Process has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Maintainers acknowledge your report (typically within 1-7 days for active projects). Then: They may ask follow-up questions in the private advisory thread. Next: They develop and test a fix. Last: They request a CVE (Common Vulnerability and Exposure) number from GitHub. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave The Responsible Disclosure Process, what is the practical point?

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: They publish a new release with the fix; They publish a public security advisory - you may be credited as the reporter; and The CVE is published publicly (usually 90 days after report or after the fix is released). The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on What If the Maintainer Doesn't Respond? What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the move inside What If the Maintainer Doesn't Respond?: if a maintainer doesn't respond within a reasonable time (30-90 days is the standard window).

speaker: ALEX
text: Walk it in order: Send a follow-up in the private advisory thread; Contact GitHub directly if the issue is critical (GitHub can assist with coordinated disclosure); and Follow the project's SECURITY.md for their stated disclosure policy. The sequence works because every action has a confirmation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a useful checkpoint before anyone starts pressing keys.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Checkpoints turn uncertainty into information.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the thread going. Anchor this part on 6. The SECURITY.md File. A SECURITY.md file at the repository root defines the project's security policy. This is the part to say slowly: accessibility-agents's security policy: community-access/accessibility-agents has a SECURITY.md that points to GitHub's private advisory form.

speaker: ALEX
text: The room should hear these as checkpoints. Supported versions: Which versions receive security patches. Reporting instructions: How to report a vulnerability (email, private advisory form, etc.). Response timeline: How quickly maintainers aim to respond. Disclosure policy: When the project will publish a fix publicly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Navigating to a Repository's SECURITY.md. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Navigating to a Repository's SECURITY.md has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: The path is straightforward once it is named. Step one is security and quality tab → Policies section → "Security policy" link. Step two is or directly: https://github.com/owner/repo/security/policy. Pause after each step and listen for the confirmation before moving on.

speaker: ALEX
text: Treat examples as spoken recipes, not decorations. You may hear something like Security and quality tab → H → "Policy" heading → Link: "Security policy" → Enter. Read the command, understand what it changes, then run it only when the repository state matches the lesson.

speaker: ALEX
text: Another way to ground it. Do not treat 7. Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) as decoration. An SBOM is a machine-readable inventory of every component (libraries, packages, dependencies) in a software project. That is not trivia. It is increasingly required by enterprise and government organizations for supply chain security compliance. The workshop is closer to rehearsal than lecture. You hear the move, try the move, and then check what changed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Exporting an SBOM from GitHub. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Exporting an SBOM from GitHub has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: If someone is taking notes, this is the short list. Your organization requires SBOM documentation before adopting an open source dependency. You're auditing a project's complete dependency chain. You want to identify license compatibility for a commercial product.

speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Navigate to the repository's Insights tab. Then: Select "Dependency graph". Next: Select "Export SBOM" button (top right of the Dependency graph page). Last: GitHub generates a SPDX-format JSON file listing all dependencies with their versions and licenses. Each step should leave a trace you can name.

speaker: ALEX
text: This is the part worth saying out loud. Put 9. Security and Accessibility Agents into plain language. Accessibility Agents' /security-dashboard slash command gives you a quick security overview without visiting the Security and quality tab in the browser.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that because it gives people permission to slow down.

speaker: ALEX
text: That is the goal. We want the page to feel explorable, not fragile.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Sample output. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Next: Appendix Q: GitHub Actions Back: Appendix O: Branch Protection Teaching chapter: Chapter 08: Open Source Culture.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should people carry with them after this?

speaker: ALEX
text: Carry the map. Know what page or tool you are in, know which action you are taking, and know what confirmation should follow. If the confirmation is missing, pause. That pause is not wasted time; it is professional judgment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a better way to say it than just follow the steps.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Steps matter, but understanding wins. That is episode 29. Next in the series is episode 30, where we keep building the same contributor muscles.
